content:
{
  "title": "Adaptive Test-Driven Prompting for Improved LLM Code Generation",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate syntactically correct but semantically incorrect code, especially on complex programming tasks. Standard prompts fail to guide models toward code that passes relevant test cases.",
  "motivation": "Current prompting strategies do not sufficiently leverage feedback from test cases, limiting code correctness and robustness in LLM outputs. As LLMs are deployed for increasingly challenging software engineering tasks, better alignment with functional requirements is critical.",
  "method": "We propose 'Adaptive Test-Driven Prompting' (ATDP), a method that incorporates dynamically selected unit test feedback into the LLM's prompt. Given a code generation task, the prompt first presents the function signature, a brief docstring, and a diverse set of test cases. After the initial code is generated, failed test cases are identified (either using an automated validator or simulated feedback), and their failure messages are appended to a follow-up prompt. The LLM is then re-prompted to revise the code specifically to address these failures, iteratively if needed. This prompt structure explicitly conditions the model on functional correctness signals.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate ATDP on HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing against standard and instruction-based prompts. Use code pass@k and exact match accuracy as primary metrics. Include ablations (e.g., static vs. adaptive test inclusion) and analyze improvements on multi-step or error-prone problems."
}
